metadata.title: The Discriminant — Three Parabolas, Three Stories

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Konnichiwa, Liam — in for Bear. The discriminant — three parabolas, three stories? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: The discriminant — three parabolas, three stories?

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. The discriminant — three parabolas, three stories.

The discriminant b squared minus four a c is not just a formula to memorize. It is a diagnostic: plug in the coefficients and the sign tells you exactly how many real solutions exist before you do any algebra. One number, three possible outcomes.

claude — write a Manim scene showing three parabolas side by side: one with discriminant greater than zero giving 2 roots, one with discriminant equal to zero giving 1 root, one with discriminant less than zero giving 0 real roots, and show the discriminant value below each

props.command:
claude "write a Manim scene showing three parabolas:
  D>0: 2 real roots  (x^2-3x+2)
  D=0: 1 real root   ((x-1)^2)
  D<0: 0 real roots  (x^2+x+1)
  show discriminant value below each"

beats.narration_text: The CLI writes it. discriminant_plots.py — three cases. First: a equals 1, b equals negative 3, c equals 2 — discriminant equals 1, positive, 2 roots. Second: a equals 1, b equals negative 2, c equals 1 — discriminant equals 0, 1 root. Third: a equals 1, b equals 1, c equals 1 — discriminant equals negative 3, no real roots.

props.code:
# discriminant_plots.py — Claude Code output
cases = [
    (1, -3, 2, "2 roots"),   # D=9-8=1>0
    (1, -2, 1, "1 root"),    # D=4-4=0
    (1,  1, 1, "0 real roots"),  # D=1-4=-3<0
]
for a, b, c, desc in cases:
    D = b**2 - 4*a*c
    print(f"a={a},b={b},c={c}: D={D} → {desc}")

beats.narration_text: Run it. Three parabolas appear side by side. Left: crosses the x-axis twice — discriminant positive. Center: touches the x-axis at exactly one point — discriminant zero. Right: does not touch the x-axis — discriminant negative. Labels confirm the story below each.
claude — add the quadratic formula with the discriminant highlighted and arrow pointing to each case

props.command:
claude "add the quadratic formula with the
  discriminant highlighted and arrow
  pointing to each case"

beats.narration_text: The quadratic formula appears at the top. A gold box highlights b squared minus 4ac — the discriminant. An arrow points down. Three cases list below: delta greater than zero gives two distinct real roots; delta equals zero gives one repeated real root; delta less than zero gives no real roots.

The discriminant saves algebra. Before solving, compute it. If it is negative, stop — there are no real solutions and the problem requires complex numbers. If it is zero, expect a repeated root and a perfect square. The formula is telling you the answer's type before you know the answer itself.

For each quadratic in your textbook, compute only the discriminant before solving. Predict the number of real roots. Then solve and verify. You will find the discriminant is consistently right — and faster than guessing.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
